hook: You already know how to compute a p-value and decide whether to reject a null hypothesis — but have you ever wondered whether the test you're using is actually the *best possible* test? It turns out that 'best' has a precise mathematical meaning, and not every test earns that title. In this unit, you'll build the theoretical machinery to prove optimality, construct powerful tests from first principles, and connect the abstract theory back to the confidence intervals and sample-size formulas you use every day.
concept: Every topic here addresses the same core question: given your data and hypotheses, how do you make the most efficient decision possible while controlling error? The unifying framework is the formal optimization of statistical decision rules, captured by the principle of the *most powerful critical region*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to prove that the likelihood ratio test is the most powerful test of fixed size $\alpha$ for simple hypotheses, giving you the gold standard against which all other tests are measured.
Extend the Neyman-Pearson result to composite hypotheses by identifying when a single test dominates all competitors simultaneously across an entire parameter range.
Apply the generalized likelihood ratio statistic $\Lambda$ to construct practical tests when no UMP test exists, covering the broad class of multi-parameter and composite hypothesis problems.
Master the duality between hypothesis tests and confidence intervals, using the $t$-distribution to build exact intervals for a normal mean when the population variance is unknown.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Power of a Test and Sample Size Calculations
topic_roadmap.description: Calculate the power function $\beta(\theta)$ of a test and determine the minimum sample size needed to detect a meaningful effect with a pre-specified level of power.
Apply the asymptotic $\chi^2$ distribution of $-2\ln\Lambda$ to perform likelihood ratio tests in realistic settings where exact distributions are unavailable.
